This brute force attack is the most straightforward and unsophisticated form of cyberattack in existence, relying entirely on computational power and persistence rather than cleverness or exploitation of any specific vulnerability. The attacker simply tries every possible combination of characters, passwords, or keys in sequence until the correct one is found, the same way a person trying to open a combination lock with no knowledge of the code would systematically try 000, 001, 002, and work their way through every possibility until the lock opens. In the digital context this process is automated with software that can attempt thousands, millions, or billions of combinations per second depending on the hardware being used and the complexity of what is being cracked. Simple four digit PINs can be brute forced in seconds, common passwords drawn from dictionary wordlists can be cracked in minutes, but a truly random password of sufficient length becomes computationally infeasible to crack because the number of possible combinations exceeds what any realistic amount of hardware could process in a human lifetime.  The effectiveness of a brute force attack is almost entirely determined by two factors working against each other: the complexity of the target credential and the speed at which the attacker can make attempts. This is why modern security systems implement rate limiting, account lockouts after a defined number of failed attempts, and CAPTCHA challenges, because these measures reduce the attacker's attempt speed from millions per second down to a handful per minute, making even a moderately complex password effectively uncrackable through brute force alone. The rise of GPU-accelerated password cracking changed the calculus significantly because graphics cards can perform the parallel mathematical operations required for password hashing at speeds thousands of times faster than a CPU, which is why an eight character password that would have taken years to crack in 2005 can now be broken in hours with modern hardware. The practical defense is not just password complexity but the combination of length, randomness, rate limiting, and multi-factor authentication that together make the time and resource cost of a successful brute force attack economically and practically irrational for an attacker to pursue.

ATT&CK framework  can be overwhelming at first due to the vast amount of information available but don't let that stop you. 😌 I just completed the MITRE ATT&CK room on @tryhackme, and here is what I learned.  ATT&CK is built around three core concepts. Tactic, which is an attacker's goal or objective. Let's say an attacker wants to perform reconnaissance on their target; that is the tactic. Technique is how they achieve it. They may utilize active scanning to achieve their reconnaissance goal. And lastly, sub-techniques, which goes deeper. For instance, active scanning comprises three specific methods: scanning IP blocks, vulnerability scanning, and wordlist scanning. I also learnt about the ATT&CK Matrix and Navigator. I used a Navigator to analyze real threat groups and their TTPs.  #Cybersecurity #TryHackMe #LearningInPublic

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗘𝘃𝗲𝗻𝘁  On April 14, 2026, as U.S. and Israeli missiles were launched toward Isfahan, Iran’s national core network—the backbone of its government, military communication, and civilian infrastructure (primarily Cisco, Fortinet, and Juniper)—completely paralyzed at the same time as the missile attack.  • 𝗜𝗿𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗠𝗲𝗱𝗶𝗮: Claimed the country was the victim of "deep sabotage". They alleged that American-made "black boxes" (routers) failed at the "zero hour" of the attack, preventing emergency teams from coordinating a response.   • 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗻𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: At the time of the April 14 strikes, Iran’s National Information Network (NIN) had already been disconnected from the global "World Wide Web" for weeks due to domestic security protocols.  • 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗟𝗼𝗴𝗶𝗰: If the "front door" (the global internet) was locked and barred, a traditional hacker sitting in Langley or Tel Aviv could not have "broken in" to crash the system in real-time.  • 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝗰𝗹𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻: Therefore, the failure must have been pre-programmed. The "poison" wasn't sent over the internet on the day of the attack; it was already inside the house, waiting for a specific internal trigger or a timed "logic bomb" embedded in the hardware's own "DNA".  • 𝗙𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗲𝗱 𝗥𝗲𝗰𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆: Traditional emergency measures, such as rebooting or re-flashing firmware, were completely ineffective because the "poison" was embedded at the hardware/chip level.  • 𝗙𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗔𝘁𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗣𝗮𝘁𝗵𝘀: Reports identify four specific methods used:  1) 𝗙𝗶𝗿𝗺𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗕𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗱𝗼𝗼𝗿𝘀: Hardware-level access that doesn't require an internet connection.  2) 𝗠𝗮𝗹𝗶𝗰𝗶𝗼𝘂𝘀 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮 𝗣𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗲𝘁𝘀: "Logic bombs" released within the internal network.  3) 𝗟𝘂𝗿𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗧𝗿𝗼𝗷𝗮𝗻𝘀: Dormant "sleeper" code activated by a specific signal.  4) 𝗦𝘂𝗽𝗽𝗹𝘆 𝗖𝗵𝗮𝗶𝗻 𝗣𝗼𝗹𝗹𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: Poisoning components during the manufacturing process.  • 𝗙𝗶𝗿𝗺𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗕𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗱𝗼𝗼𝗿𝘀 & 𝗦𝘂𝗽𝗽𝗹𝘆 𝗖𝗵𝗮𝗶𝗻 𝗣𝗼𝗹𝗹𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: Today, it is a known technical reality that malicious code can be inserted into the UEFI/BIOS or the BMC (Baseboard Management Controller) of networking hardware during manufacturing. Organizations like Eclypsium have documented "Jaguar Tooth" malware targeting Cisco IOS and persistent vulnerabilities in Fortinet and Juniper devices that resist standard reboots or software patches.  • 𝗧𝗵𝗲 "𝗚𝗲𝗻𝗲" 𝗔𝗻𝗮𝗹𝗼𝗴𝘆: In technical terms, this refers to Hardware-Root-of-Trust compromise. If the silicon itself (the "gene") or the low-level firmware is compromised, the device is essentially a "Trojan Horse" that can be deactivated by a specific "logic bomb" or signal, regardless of how many times you restart it.  • 𝗖𝘂𝗿𝗿𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗦𝘂𝘀𝗽𝗶𝗰𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀: While western manufacturers deny these "kill switches," the general suspicion of supply chain pollution is what drove the U.S. and its allies to ban Huawei and ZTE. The video’s claim is effectively the "mirror image" of this logic applied to U.S. brands like Cisco and Juniper.

A simple software update might patch a vulnerability, but as big tech companies keep layering on more patches and features, the codebase grows increasingly complex. Over time, it becomes harder and harder to find flaws — until the system feels almost bulletproof. That is, until someone with a quantum computer steps in. I’m not joking: quantum computing could eventually decipher even the most hardened code, because locating subtle bugs in a billion-dollar company’s software is already extremely difficult today. And in historically critical moments, the odds of undetected weaknesses are far lower than most people expect.  But ofc I don’t believe it’s a Time Machine thing going on.

[숨겨진 욕구 — 7가지 숨겨진 취약점]  인간은 스스로 인식하지 못하는 욕구들을 가지고 있습니다. 그리고 그 욕구들이 건드려지는 순간, 의식적 판단보다 훨씬 빠르게 반응합니다. 다음의 일곱 가지는 그 숨겨진 취약점들입니다.  1. 필요로 하는 욕구 모든 사람은 자신이 필요한 존재임을 알 때 긍정적으로 반응합니다. 누군가에게 없어서는 안 될 존재라는 감각은, 어떤 논리적 설득보다 강하게 사람을 움직입니다. 이 욕구가 건드려지는 순간 사람은 스스로도 모르게 그 관계 안으로 깊이 들어오게 됩니다.  2. 희망에 대한 욕구 곤경에 처했을 때 사람은 희망을 발견하면 거기에 집중합니다. 추가로 그 희망이 이루어질 것이라는 확신이 들면 완전히 매몰됩니다. 희망은 단순한 긍정적 감정이 아닙니다. 출구가 보이지 않는 상태에서 제시되는 희망은, 그것이 아무리 작더라도 사람의 모든 에너지를 그 방향으로 집중시킵니다.  3. 희생양에 대한 욕구 이 숨겨진 욕구가 강력한 이유는 명확합니다. 내면을 들여다보고 자신이 책임져야 할 부분을 찾는 것보다, 다른 사람이나 다른 것을 비난하는 것이 훨씬 쉽고 스트레스 해소까지 되기 때문입니다. 도덕적 사회에서의 내로남불, 에셈 문화 안에서의 변바 찾기가 바로 그와 같은 것입니다. 비난할 대상이 생기는 순간 사람은 일시적으로 자신이 옳은 편에 서 있다는 안도감을 얻습니다. 그리고 그 안도감은 중독적입니다.  4. 주목받고 이해받고 싶은 욕구 사람들은 자신이 주목받고 있다고 느낄 때 중요하다고 느낍니다. 단순히 칭찬을 받는 것이 아닙니다. 누군가가 자신을 제대로 바라보고 있다는 감각, 자신의 이야기가 진지하게 들려지고 있다는 느낌. 그것만으로도 사람은 그 대상에게 깊은 신뢰와 애착을 형성하기 시작합니다.  5. 자신이 모르는 것을 알고 싶은 욕구 비밀을 갖는 것은 누군가를 특별하게 만듭니다. 그 비밀이 부여하는 추가적인 지식과 힘을 암시하기 때문에 사람들은 무의식적으로 몸을 앞으로 기울이고 귀를 기울이며 집중하면서 그것을 가져가려 합니다. 인간은 알지 않아도 될 것을 알고 싶은 본능적 호기심을 가지고 있습니다. 그리고 그런 비밀은 자신의 자원이 된다고 느끼며, 힘이 생긴다는 감각을 갖습니다. 비밀이 쉽게 새나가는 이유도 바로 여기에 있습니다.  6. 옳다고 느끼고 싶은 욕구 사람들은 자신이 옳다고 느끼는 데 중독감을 느낍니다. 설령 자신이 틀렸다고 해도 상관이 없습니다. 좋아요가 많으면 기분이 좋아지는 것도 그런 이유 중 하나입니다. "좋아요 누를 거야, 안 누를 거야?"라는 작은 선택조차도 자신이 옳은 편에 서 있다는 확인 욕구와 연결되어 있습니다. 이 욕구는 논쟁에서 지지 않으려는 집착, 사과하지 못하는 태도, 끝없는 자기 합리화의 근원이기도 합니다.  7. 힘을 느끼고 싶은 욕구 자기 통제력을 느낄 수 있는 기회가 주어지면 사람들은 긍정적으로 반응합니다. 자기 통제감은 에너지를 솟게 만들고 기분을 좋게 합니다. 그러나 흥미로운 것은 그 반대편에도 쾌락이 존재한다는 것입니다. 제한된 한도 내에서의 무력감, 즉 통제를 내려놓는 것 그 자체도 쾌락이 될 수 있습니다. 이것이 지배와 피지배에서 섭이 느끼는 복종의 쾌락과 정확히 맞닿아 있습니다. 통제하는 쾌락과 통제를 내어주는 쾌락, 이 두 가지는 서로 반대 방향에 있지만 동일한 욕구의 두 얼굴입니다.

그래서 이게 어떤 건데?  일부는 이미 4월 10일부터 실제 해커들이 공격에 쓰고 있습니다.  이 취약점이 뚫리면 실제로 무슨 일이 벌어지냐면:  · 해커가 내 PC에 들어와서 가장 먼저 백신 종료. · 백신이 꺼진 상태에서 랜섬웨어 설치.   (랜섬웨어 = 내 모든 파일을 암호로 잠가버리는 악성코드) · 가족 사진, 업무 파일, 졸업논문, 계약서 = 모두 열람 불가 · 풀어주는 대가로 보통 비트코인 수백만 원을 요구합니다 · 돈을 줘도 풀어준다는 보장은 없습니다  3개 취약점 중 1개(BlueHammer)만 막혔고, 나머지 2개는 아직 막을 방법이 없습니다. 미국 사이버보안 정부기관(CISA)이 모든 연방기관에 5월 6일까지 패치를 강제했습니다. 이 정도로 위험하다는 신호입니다.  지금 당장 할 일: [설정 → 업데이트 및 보안 → Windows 업데이트] 확인 "최신 상태입니다"가 떠야 안전합니다.  ⚠️회사·기관 보안 담당자인 레이디라면 월요일 출근 직후 점검 필수⚠️

🇮🇩 Indonesia Government Data Leak — Jombang Bapenda  A threat actor claims to have leaked data from Jombang Regional Revenue Agency (bapenda.jombangkab.go.id) in East Java, Indonesia.  The dataset reportedly contains ~100,000 records (2025–2026) in CSV format.  Exposed data includes:  Citizen names Phone numbers Service request details (tax/administrative processes) Application types and processing status Location data (village/district level) Internal handling metadata (entry officer, timelines, notes)  This system appears to manage public service and tax-related requests, making the data highly sensitive for both citizens and government operations.  ⚠️ Status: Unverified — based on underground forum activity ⚠️ Risk includes targeted scams, impersonation, and abuse of government service workflows  #DDW #DataLeak #Indonesia #CyberSecurity #DarkWeb #DataBreach #Infosec

🇵🇪 🎓 Peru Government Data Leak — Education Sector  A threat actor claims to have leaked data from the Regional Education Directorate of Madre de Dios (dredmdd.gob.pe) in Peru.  The dump reportedly contains two databases linked to administrative and educational systems.  Exposed systems may include:  Recruitment and job application records Administrative document submissions Payroll and employee-related data (including digital pay stubs) Institutional and operational records  The platform is used for managing education services, hiring processes, and internal government operations in the region.  ⚠️ Status: Unverified — based on underground forum activity ⚠️ Potential risks include exposure of employee data, applicants’ personal information, and internal government records  #DDW #DataLeak #Peru #CyberSecurity #DarkWeb #DataBreach #Infosec

🇬🇹 Guatemala Government Data Exposure — 200K+ Users  A threat actor claims to have accessed the Guatemala Ministry of Labor and Social Welfare (tuempleo.mintrabajo.gob.gt) via an unsecured API.  The exposed data reportedly includes 200,000+ user records along with ~40GB of CVs and internal documents.  Compromised information allegedly contains:  Full names, date of birth, and national ID (DPI) Phone numbers, email addresses, and home addresses Employment history, salary, and job applications Uploaded resumes (CVs) and internal system data  The actor claims the system had no authentication or access controls, allowing full data extraction.  ⚠️ Status: Unverified — based on underground forum activity ⚠️ Potential impact is severe, including identity theft, fraud, and large-scale social engineering  #DDW #DataLeak #Guatemala #CyberSecurity #DarkWeb #DataBreach #Infosec
